title: 本周应用：Quibi死亡，Snapchat飙升，iPhone 12哈利德升级
title_en: This Week in Apps: Quibi dies, Snapchat soars, Halide upgrades for iPhone 12

note:
Facebook Inc.。该公司要求纽约大学(New York University)的一个研究项目停止收集有关其政治广告定向操作的数据，这引发了一场与未经该公司许可而试图研究该平台的学者的斗争。
这场纠纷涉及纽约大学广告观察站(NYU Ad Observatory)，该项目由纽约大学工程学院于上月启动，招募了6500多名志愿者，让他们使用一个专门设计的浏览器扩展来收集Facebook向他们展示的政治广告的数据。
在10月16日发给纽约大学广告观察站(NYU Ad Observatory)研究人员的一封信中，Facebook表示，该项目违反了服务条款中禁止从其网站收集大量数据的条款。
这封由Facebook隐私政策官员艾莉森·亨德里克斯(Allison Hendrix)写的信中写道，无论多么善意的抓取工具，都不是从我们这里收集信息的允许手段。她写道，如果学校不结束该项目并删除它收集的数据，你可能会受到额外的强制措施。
这家社交媒体巨头与一所主要研究型大学之间的冲突发生在下个月美国大选之前，社交媒体上的政治广告受到了更严格的审查。Facebook最近几周表示，将在选举日之前禁止新的政治广告，并在当晚无限期暂停所有政治广告，以防止有关选举结果的有偿错误信息的传播。
继2016年总统竞选中政治广告的不透明性引发轩然大波后，Facebook推出了一个在其平台上播放的广告档案，其中包括谁为广告付费，广告播放的时间，以及观看广告的人的地理位置等信息。但该资料库排除了有关确定谁会看到广告的目标信息。
纽约大学广告观察站(NYU Ad Observatory)背后的研究人员表示，他们希望为记者、研究人员、政策制定者和其他人提供按州搜索政治广告的能力，并进行竞争，看看哪些信息是针对特定受众的，以及这些广告是如何获得资金的。
Facebook要求该项目停止收集信息，遭到了支持提高广告透明度的人士的反对，其中包括明尼苏达州民主党参议员艾米·克洛布查尔(Amy Klobuchar)，她是一项名为“诚实广告法案”(Honest Adds Act)的法案的发起人之一，该法案将要求提高在线政治广告的透明度。
克洛布查尔在给“华尔街日报”的一份声明中说，在选举进行到一半时，Facebook让美国人更难获得有关在线政治广告的信息，这是不可接受的。她说，社交媒体平台已经承诺让在线广告更加透明，但Facebook威胁要对纽约大学采取行动，这进一步证明了自愿标准是不够的。
Facebook表示，与传统媒体或竞争对手的社交平台相比，它已经为政治广告提供了更高的透明度，从用户的平台活动中自动收集数据--即使是在他们允许的情况下--构成了不可接受的隐私威胁。
Facebook发言人乔·奥斯本在给“华尔街日报”的一份声明中说：我们几个月前就通知纽约大学，推进一个窃取人们Facebook信息的项目将违反我们的条款，并补充说，如果该项目不自愿关闭，Facebook可能会对自己的代码进行技术修改，从而阻止纽约大学的研究人员收集数据。
对于Facebook来说，允许外人访问其平台上的数据一直是一个棘手的领域。剑桥分析(Cambridge Analytica)是一家在2016年获得未经授权访问Facebook用户数据用于政治侧写的公司，在此引发轩然大波后，联邦贸易委员会(Federal Trade Commission)推动Facebook控制第三方数据访问。Facebook对外界获取、分析和使用从其平台收集的数据的能力施加了一系列限制。该公司提出了法律要求，有时还对其指控为邪恶目的寻求数据访问的实体提起诉讼。
近年来，对社交媒体数据抓取的哪些限制是可以强制执行的，这一直是诉讼的主题，各平台辩称，它们既有权利也有责任防止未经授权使用用户生成的数据。
纽约大学的项目已经收集了20多万个广告背后的定向数据。研究人员表示，这暴露了Facebook在2016年大选后创建的可公开获取的政治广告档案未能记录本应在系统中的广告的领域。
Facebook表示，它赞赏纽约大学研究人员为改善广告库所做的努力，但不会代表违反其规则。
纽约大学坦登工程学院(Tandon School Of Engineering)研究员劳拉·埃德尔森(Laura Edelson)帮助监督了广告观察站项目，她说，唯一能促使我们停止做这件事的是Facebook是否会自己做这件事，而我们已经呼吁他们这样做。
Facebook写给纽约大学的信为其向外部研究人员提供信息的努力进行了辩护，指出该公司已经建立了官方的学术合作伙伴关系，以研究该网站在2020年美国大选期间对选民的影响。
乔治华盛顿大学(George Washington University)数据、民主和政治研究所(Institute for Data，Democratic and Politics at George Washington University)所长丽贝卡·特罗姆布尔(Rebekah Tromble)表示，Facebook自己的研究举措值得称赞，但她补充说，她不同意Facebook针对纽约大学项目采取的行动。
乔治敦大学数据、民主和政治研究所(Institute for Data，Democratic and Politics)副所长特伦布尔说，Facebook的“围墙花园”后面关闭了太多的关键信息。而像广告观察站这样的努力在打破这些围墙方面起到了关键作用。
本文已于晚上8：22更正。ET反映，Rebekah Tromble是乔治华盛顿大学数据、民主和政治研究所所长。最初的版本错误地说她的头衔是副主任，并错误地将学校误认为乔治敦大学。

note_en:
Facebook Inc. is demanding that a New York University research project cease collecting data about its political-ad targeting practices, setting up a fight with academics seeking to study the platform without the company's permission.
 The dispute involves the NYU Ad Observatory, a project launched last month by the university's engineering school that has recruited more than 6,500 volunteers to use a specially designed browser extension to collect data about the political ads Facebook shows them.
 In a letter sent Oct. 16 to the researchers behind the NYU Ad Observatory, Facebook said the project violates provisions in its terms of service that prohibit bulk data collection from its site.
 "Scraping tools, no matter how well-intentioned, are not a permissible means of collecting information from us," said the letter, written by a Facebook privacy policy official, Allison Hendrix. If the university doesn't end the project and delete the data it has collected, she wrote, "you may be subject to additional enforcement action."
 The clash between the social-media giant and a major research university comes at a time of heightened scrutiny over political advertising on social media ahead of next month's U.S. election. Facebook in recent weeks has said it would bar new political ads ahead of Election Day and suspend all political ads indefinitely that evening to prevent the spread of paid misinformation about the election outcome.
 Following a furor about the opaque nature of political advertising in the 2016 presidential campaign, Facebook launched an archive of advertisements that run on its platform, with information such as who paid for an ad, when it ran and the geographic location of people who saw it. But that library excludes information about the targeting that determines who sees the ads.
 The researchers behind the NYU Ad Observatory said they wanted to provide journalists, researchers, policy makers, and others with the ability to search political ads by state and contest to see what messages are targeted to specific audiences and how those ads are funded.
 Facebook's demand that the project stop its collection drew opposition from proponents of greater ad transparency, including Sen. Amy Klobuchar (D., Minn.), a sponsor of a bill called the Honest Ads Act that would mandate greater transparency in online political advertising.
 "It's unacceptable that in the middle of an election, Facebook is making it harder for Americans to get information about online political ads," Ms. Klobuchar said in a statement to The Wall Street Journal. Social media platforms have pledged to make online advertising more transparent, she said, but Facebook's threatened action against NYU "is further evidence that voluntary standards are insufficient."
 Facebook said it already offers more transparency into political advertising than either traditional media or rival social platforms, and that the automated collection of data from users' on-platform activity -- even with their permission -- poses an unacceptable privacy threat.
 "We informed NYU months ago that moving forward with a project to scrape people's Facebook information would violate our terms," Facebook spokesman Joe Osborne said in a statement to the Journal, adding that if the project doesn't shut down voluntarily, Facebook could make technical changes to its own code that would block the NYU researchers from collecting data.
 For Facebook, allowing outsiders to access data on its platform has been tricky territory. Following the uproar over Cambridge Analytica, a company that obtained unauthorized access to Facebook user data for political profiling in 2016, the Federal Trade Commission pushed Facebook to rein in third-party data access. Facebook imposed a series of restrictions on outsiders' ability to obtain, analyze and use data gathered from its platforms. The company has sent legal demands and sometimes filed suits against entities it accuses of seeking data access for nefarious purposes.
 What limitations on social media data scraping are enforceable has been the subject of litigation in recent years, with platforms arguing they have both a right and responsibility to prevent the unauthorized use of user-generated data.
 The NYU project has already collected the targeting data behind more than 200,000 ads. Researchers say it has exposed areas where the publicly available archive of political ads Facebook created after the 2016 election is failing to log advertisements that should be in the system.
 Facebook said it has appreciated the NYU researchers' efforts to improve the ad library, but won't stand for violations of its rules.
 Laura Edelson, a researcher at NYU's Tandon School of Engineering who helps oversee the Ad Observatory project, said, "The only thing that would prompt us to stop doing this would be if Facebook would do it themselves, which we have called on them to do."
 Facebook's letter to NYU defended its efforts to make information available to outside researchers, noting that the company has set up an official academic partnership to study the site's impact on voters during the 2020 U.S. election.
 Rebekah Tromble, the director of the Institute for Data, Democracy and Politics at George Washington University, said Facebook deserves credit for its own research initiatives, but added that she disagrees with its action against the NYU project.
 "There's far too much critical information closed up behind Facebook's walled garden," said Ms. Tromble, associate director of Georgetown's Institute for Data, Democracy, and Politics. "And efforts like the Ad Observatory play a critical role in breaking down those walls."
    This article was corrected at 8:22 p.m. ET to reflect that Rebekah Tromble is the director of the Institute for Data, Democracy and Politics at George Washington University. The original version incorrectly said her title is associate director and misidentified the school as Georgetown University.

title: Facebook要求关闭针对政治广告的研究项目
title_en: Facebook Demands Shutdown of Research Project Into Its Targeting of Political Ads

note:
科技公司Output的软件已被德雷克(Drake)、酷玩乐队(Coldplay)、贾斯汀·比伯(Justin Bieber)和蕾哈娜(Rihanna)使用在歌曲中，音乐配乐包括Stranger Things、Game Of Thrones和Black Panther。Output从Summit Partners筹集了4500万美元的首轮投资。
这家总部位于洛杉矶的公司于2013年由首席执行官格雷格·莱曼(Gregg Lehrman)创立，他是ASCAP获奖作曲家，在汉斯·齐默(Hans Zimmer)的领导下起步，后来为各种音乐软件平台制作声音。
Output为音乐家、作曲家、制作人和声音设计师开发软件，除了通过其Arade应用程序提供的样本和循环外，还包括虚拟乐器和FX。该公司还生产工作室家具。
该公司声称其软件被制作人、词曲作者和作曲家用来“寻找灵感，突破作者的障碍”。
使用Output软件的艺术家范围从Diplo和Zazd到Mike Dean，Lil Jon和许多其他人。
根据一份宣布新投资的新闻稿，Output的旗舰音乐创作应用Arade在167个国家使用，最初的目的是“帮助音乐专业人士将一个拥有数万个循环和声音的库变成曲目”。
Output声称超过50%的Arade会话会导致新曲目的写入，并且“平均用户在15分钟内锁定一个曲目”。
此外，Output表示，自2018年以来，已有超过42万名音乐家得到Arade平台的支持，在过去12个月中，该公司的每月经常性收入(MRR)增长了300%。
这笔少数股权投资代表了Output有史以来的首次融资，并将支持该公司在全球范围内的持续增长和数字生产平台的扩张，包括在2021年增加可播放乐器和移动应用程序。
Output声称是这种规模的首批SaaS解决方案之一，在一个统一的音乐创作平台上提供循环、样本和旋律内容。
“技术使视频和照片的创作过程民主化。技术的采用将过渡到音乐制作，这是有道理的。“。
“技术使视频和照片的创作过程民主化。Output创始人兼首席执行官雷曼表示：“技术采用将过渡到音乐制作，这是有道理的。
我们正在努力用一个平台激励新一代音乐制作人，这个平台可以把录音室的力量带给任何人，无论他们在哪里。顶峰公司在消费技术、数字媒体和电子商务方面的深厚经验使他们成为帮助加速我们的全球影响力的理想合作伙伴。“。
已经加入Output董事会的Summit Partners总经理安德鲁·柯林斯(Andrew Collins)补充说：“我们相信，Output处于数字音乐创作的前沿，为各级音乐制作人提供了一个真正优雅的专业级平台。
格雷格和团队建立了一种创新的公司文化和充满活力的音乐家社区-他们以一种深思熟虑、资本效率高的方式做到了这一切。我们很高兴能与他们合作实现下一阶段的增长。“。全球音乐业务
note_en:
Output, a tech company whose software has been used in songs by Drake, Coldplay, Justin Bieber and Rihanna and musical scores including Stranger Things, Game of Thrones and Black Panther – has raised a $45 million Series A investment from Summit Partners.
 The Los Angeles-based company was founded in 2013 by CEO Gregg Lehrman, an ASCAP-award winning composer who got his start under Hans Zimmer and later produced sounds for various music software platforms.
  Output develops software for musicians, composers, producers and sound designers, including virtual instruments and FX in addition to samples and loops available through its Arcade app. The company also makes studio furniture.
 The company claims that its software is used by producers, songwriters and composers to “find inspiration and break through writer’s block”.
 The  artists using Outputs’s software range from Diplo and Zedd to Mike Dean, Lil Jon and many others.
  According to a press release announcing the new investment, Output’s flagship music creation application Arcade is used in 167 countries and was originally “intended to help music professionals turn a library of tens of thousands loops and sounds into tracks”.
 Output claims that more than 50% of Arcade sessions result in the writing of a new track and that the “average user locks in a track within fifteen minutes”.
 In addition, Output states that more than 420,000 musicians have been supported by the Arcade platform since 2018 and that the company has seen a 300% increase in monthly recurring revenue (MRR) in the last 12 months.
 This minority investment represents Output’s first-ever fundraise and will support the company’s continued global growth and expansion of its digital production platform, including the addition of playable instruments and mobile applications in 2021.
 Output claims to be one of the first SaaS solutions at this scale to offer loops, samples and melodic content in one unified platform for music creation.
 “Technology democratized the creation process for video and photos. It makes sense that technology adoption would transition to music making.”
 “Technology democratized the creation process for video and photos. It makes sense that technology adoption would transition to music making,” said Lehrman, Output Founder and CEO.
 “We are working to inspire a new generation of music makers with a platform that brings the power of a studio to anyone, wherever they are. Summit’s deep experience in consumer technology, digital media and e-commerce make them an ideal partner to help accelerate our global impact.”
  Andrew Collins, a Managing Director with Summit Partners who has joined the Output Board of Directors, added: “We believe Output is at the forefront of digital music creation, offering a truly elegant, professional-grade platform that is accessible to music makers at all levels.
 “Gregg and team have built an innovative company culture and a vibrant community of musicians– and they have done it all in a thoughtful, capital efficient manner. We’re thrilled to partner with them for this next phase of growth.” Music Business Worldwide
title: Output为音乐家、作曲家、制作人和声音设计师开发软件和虚拟乐器，由Summit Partners牵头筹集4500万美元的首轮融资
title_en: Output, which develops software and virtual instruments for musicians, composers, producers, and sound designers, raises $45M Series A led by Summit Partners

note:
|2019年6月6日晚上11：38太平洋标准时间这场盗版噩梦本应是技术上不可能的。好莱坞制片厂多年来一直在制定数字影院规范和认证程序，以确保军用级别的加密将保护最新上映的大片。录像，虽然仍可追溯，但从服务器上得到一个完美的数字拷贝是永远不会发生的。
除中国某地外，数字影院放映服务器No.。A15591允许盗版制作原始的高清拷贝，就在新电影在电影院上映的同一天。中宣部电影技术质量检验所的盗版追踪小组甚至给服务器起了个昵称：幽灵一号。他们的工作是找到它--并阻止它。这是他们为拿下中国有史以来最大的电影盗版团伙而进行的三年战斗的故事。
盗版成为中国的大生意随着中国成为世界上票房第二大的影院市场，以及世界上最大的影院银幕数量，电影发行的商业价值飙升。不仅对好莱坞和中国电影发行商，对本土电影盗版也是如此。盗版不仅仅是业余的激流爱好者，甚至在像共产主义中国这样一个警察严密的国家，盗版也是一种大规模的犯罪商业活动。随着智能手机的兴起，VCD(视频光盘)和DVD(数字视盘)的街头摊位很久以前就不再流行了，所以今天，在神秘网站上指向盗版电影的链接转而在微信和QQ等社交媒体渠道上交易，这些渠道都有方便的内置小额支付系统。
流媒体在中国的增长速度甚至快于影院的数量，2018年合法流媒体市场价值536亿元人民币(77亿美元)-仅比同年全国总票房少73亿元人民币(10亿美元)。随着中国电影票补贴的盛行，最新上映的高清影院以不到1美元的价格复制的诱惑可能是不可抗拒的。这样的机会是由一个复杂的、隐秘的私人电影院网络促成的，这个网络由一个无视知识产权的盗版集团提供。而大多数流传的高清拷贝都可以追溯到一个单一的来源。
幽灵1号、间谍和工程师英雄中国当局追查“幽灵”服务器的不朽努力的故事有一个值得拍电影的情节，有趣的是，这个故事是从一部间谍电影开始的。第一个遇到流氓服务器的人是2016年12月6日，电影技术质检所工程师周凌飞发现了一个来自派拉蒙影业的布拉德·皮特/玛丽昂·科蒂亚间谍惊悚片《盟军》盗版链接。图像取证测试和分析表明，盗版电影的数字水印ID为138471，播放服务器的序列号为A15591。数字影院服务器注册在镇江市京口区学府路九洲影城2号幕。
周通知了文化广电部，但一周后，工程师被告知，九州制片厂电影院已经关闭，设备销售一空-新的目的地尚不清楚。但“幻影服务器”才刚刚开始，因为20天后，五部新的盗版电影出现在网上，使用的是位于中国北部靠近北京的多山省份河北的北京数字剧院(Beijing Digital Theater)的身份。这次的片名不只是好莱坞电影，还有香港动作惊悚片“冲击波”。由于服务器无法实际定位，周通知了包括中国电影集团和华夏在内的中国三家主要发行商，指示他们将序列号为A15591的服务器列入黑名单，并阻止使用密钥库中的数字安全证书作为密钥传递消息(KDM)密钥来解密未来的电影。
如果说有什么不同的话，那就是中国在监督数字影院系统的部署和控制方面比好莱坞更严格。由于只发放了40个放映首映电影的影院运营商牌照，简单地购买放映机/服务器组合，然后与发行商达成协议放映他们的电影是不可能的。每台制造的投影仪和服务器，无论它们最终在世界上的什么地方，都出厂时都有一个唯一的序列号。每台设备的ID和数字安全证书由所有分销商或内容服务提供商(在中国受到严格控制)保存在称为受信任设备列表(TDL)中，并与特定的影院位置相匹配。
然后，发行商发送带有加密胶片的硬盘驱动器(也称为。数字影院包或DCP)发送到要放映电影的每家影院，然后单独发布一个KDM，该KDM为电影“解锁”
自2012年以来，中国当局已经使用数字影院水印成功追踪和起诉了500多起电影盗窃事件，包括“画皮”、“泰囧”和“战狼2”等大片。扬州市公安局治安保卫大队副支队长黄明哲(位于镇江市长江对岸，服务器的发源地)解释了幽灵一号是如何设法躲避了几年的寻找和阻止的，“所谓克隆，就是把普通剧场的一台服务器的数字安全证书复制到另一台服务器上，让两台服务器拥有相同的ID”，他说，“所谓克隆，就是把普通剧场的一台服务器的数字安全证书复制到另一台服务器上，让两台服务器拥有相同的ID。”“追捕幽灵1号”刚刚变成了一场“打地鼠”的游戏；将一张有效的安全证书列入黑名单，盗版者只需将另一张未列入黑名单的证书复制到A15591上即可。
(请注意：我们故意模糊了确切的方法，以免为任何可能成为海盗的人提供指导参考。)。
今年农历新年“2.15”行动(又名。春节或农历新年假期对于打击中国盗版的努力将被证明是至关重要的。从历史上看，这是为国内最大的大片预留的时间，《流浪地球》、《疯狂的外星人》等大片在数以万计的银幕上被预订。随着2019年中国电影电影业“冬天即将到来”的说法出现增长停滞，没有人能承受票房收入被盗版抢走的后果。但这显然就是发生的事情。从2月4日到10日，中国的总票房为58亿元人民币(8.39亿美元)，其中包括科幻大片“流浪地球”在短短四天内赚得的10亿元人民币(1.44亿美元)。尽管创下了这一时期的票房纪录，但上座率基本持平，只有更高的票价才使其比去年更强劲。盗版是不是也有部分责任呢？
根据在春节期间一直在监测盗版活动的中华人民共和国国家版权协会(PRC)的数据，在春节期间发布的8部电影的侵权在线链接数量达到了5.1万个。其中，不少于38900个侵权链接是高清版本，点击播放总数约为8282万次。据估计，这导致了约7.87亿元人民币(1.14亿美元)的票房损失。更值得注意的是，在发布的8部电影的27个盗版版本中，来源始终是一样的：服务器A15591或《幽灵1号》。
中国当局臭名昭著地阻止任何关于鬼魂或来世的好莱坞或外国电影在中国上映(甚至最近全是女性的“捉鬼敢死队”也是如此)，以免“宣扬邪教或迷信”。现在，他们即将对“幽灵”服务器采取更严厉的手段。
在公安部的主持下，代号为“2.15”的联合行动在元旦假期后展开。虽然中国各级公安机构、部委和警察部门处理这一问题可能会让外人感到困惑，但在协调安全行动方面，中国的效率令人难以置信。早在2.15行动之前，反盗版机构就已经完成了25起影视侵权盗版侦破案件，其中抓获犯罪嫌疑人251名，关闭盗版视频网站361个，关闭应用57个，查获高清电影盗版服务器7台。这些行动涉及的总金额为人民币2.3亿元(合3300万美元)。但与追捕1号幽灵相比，这一点就相形见绌了。
第一步是渗透盗版犯罪团伙用来出售高清拷贝链接的微信频道。电影在电影院上映仅一天后，完美的高清副本就出现在了网上，尽管电影制片人龚格尔努力加强了电影上映周围的安全措施，并有三个反盗版小组日夜监控所有社交媒体频道。根据从社交媒体渠道收集的信息(具体方法保密)，五个抓捕小组被派往鞍山和湘潭，这两个城市都位于中国中南部的湖南省，他们在那里监控主要嫌疑人。与此同时，13个较小的单位最初被派往8个省和11个市进行调查和取证。扬州市公安局共出动二百多名警力，分赴二十个省四十六个城市抓捕。
公安部一声令下，专案组突袭，抓获了马某某、马莫松、文某某、陆某某五名重点犯罪目标和一名刘姓技术员(化名)。另有59名嫌疑人被捕(一些报道称超过250人)。
扬州市公安局带记者参观证据时看到，这起案件的物证几乎足以填满整个仓储大厅。
在照片中，可以看到大量的“加密狗”堆积在物证架上按顺序排列的多个盒子里。专案组警官柯先生拿起一把小小的USB“加密狗”钥匙说：“这把钥匙是用来在线下私人影院安装播放设备的。没有它，犯罪集团的盗版电影就不能发行。超过9000个这样的加密狗尚未售出或发出。如果它蔓延，犯罪集团将开发出这么多可以播放盗版电影的盒子。这么大的数额会对中国电影市场产生很大的影响。“。
海盗们正在建立一个私人电影院的影子网络，非法放映首映电影。他们之所以能够做到这一点，要归功于被扣押的1.3万多台设备中的四台服务器中的一台--臭名昭著的“幽灵一号”。然而，A15591或“幽灵一号”服务器如何成为盗版电影的源头的故事，给国内外的电影业上了最令人不寒而栗的一课。因为没有什么能阻止2号或3号幽灵等等。
A15591服务器是如何成为中国电影发行商的最大敌人的细节首先由在线新闻门户网站搜狐详细披露，但中国新闻、Xhby等公司补充报道。以下内容是在谷歌翻译的帮助下从多个来源拼凑而成的。
主要嫌疑人马某某于2014年6月注册，与以前的同学和马莫松一起在鞍山市经营一家免下车电影院。鞍山市是中国北方的一个钢铁小镇，与英国的谢菲尔德是姊妹城市。但位于达莲以北、朝鲜以西的北方气候，风大、尘土飞扬，不利于汽车影院的上映。于是，马某和马莫松这两位老同学开始为他们的“两马”犯罪团伙构思新的商业计划。(“马”是中文中马的意思。)。2015年，他们与当地商业合作伙伴兼经纪人霍莫雷联手，在免下车影院增加了一个屏幕，可以放映新电影。
大约在这个时候，私人电影院(也就是。“微型电影院”或“点播电影院”开始在中国各地兴起，其中大多数是合法的，但许多电影院在执行从消防安全到知识产权保护的各种法规方面也不严格。但是，这样的场馆如何获得先发优势，并以常规影院发行的最新电影吸引顾客，这是马云的问题。要获得第一轮放映，就需要盗版高清晰度摄像机录制，但对于每一次影院放映来说，这都是一件危险的事情。唯一不同的是，与其使用劣质的录像带拷贝，不如直接从服务器获取原始的DCP。因此，2017年，马某某从一位名叫霍某磊的男子那里支付了5万元人民币(约合7235美元)，购买了序列号为A15591的第一代GDC数字影院服务器。这是符合DCI之前版本的服务器。此前，霍建华开了一家电影院设备经销商，为普通电影院销售电影播放设备，并提供翻新和维护服务。
这是前一年被确定为盗版电影来源的服务器，因此被正式列入TDL的黑名单，用于创建安全密钥(KDM)。于是马某某购买了一台实际上毫无用处的服务器。这就是霍某磊插手帮助解决如何从服务器获取盗版拷贝的问题。他联系了一位姓刘的技术员，这名技术员知道如何“克隆”服务器证书。刘某以“设备维修”为名进入河北唐县一家电影院，偷偷复制其服务器的数字证书，下载KDM存储服务器的账号和密码。因此，“幽灵1号”重新开始使用新克隆的序列号为A03783的安全证书。因此，一旦从盗版视频文件取证地提取了水印，就不会追溯到1号幽灵，因为图像中的不可见水印将基于TDL揭示重放设备的注册来源。
随着设备问题的解决，是时候让他们拿到一些内容了。这两匹马检查了猫眼门票应用，寻找观众得分最高的三部即将上映的影片。然后，他们与安神市一家综合影院的投影部负责人王某飞合作，王某飞成为他们的DCP供应商，这些DCP是从经销商合法发送的硬盘中提取的。为此，两匹马每月付给他500-1000元人民币(75-150美元)，让他借硬盘最多看10部电影。回到马某某的工作室，该团伙使用专业的高清摄像机和声卡来录制电影，并使用视频编辑软件对完成的电影文件进行调整、校正和同步。
盗版影院网络没有出售实体拷贝(DVD)或在网上流媒体播放，马某和马莫松决定瞄准私人电影院。截至2017年底，中国估计有8000多家这样的“点播影院”，其中许多在争夺客户时不尊重版权。该团伙采取了联系影子私人影院负责人的传统营销方式，介绍他们的业务，然后给他们提供盗版样品播放。他们用公安部治安管理局副局长张作良的话说，他们“建立了一条盗版院线电影制作、发行和加密管理的黑色产业链”。
马某和马莫松知道盗版电影没有荣誉，于是建立了一个类似正版电影院的系统，通过在网络磁盘存储上添加水印、加密和传输来监控离线使用，以确保他们的盗版电影不会被重新盗版。二号犯罪嫌疑人马莫松向警方交代称：“两年时间里，我们一共拍了200多部高清盗版电影。广告和沟通是在微信身上进行的，向记者展示了海盗团伙与一名私人电影院老板之间的交流，这名老板抱怨他拥有13个房间的私人电影院的设备成本。两匹马的联络人回答说：“月费是3000元(434美元)。第一次必须购买加密磁盘。加密盘将由快递员递送，每个房间的费用是500元人民币(72美元)，您可以稍后再使用。那么使用费是每月100元(14.50美元)。不可以，加密盘可以退还，退还300元人民币(43美元)。“。购买这些盗版高清电影的私人影院反过来向顾客做广告：“看电影，2个人只需要98元(14.18美元)”，这样算下来比电影院便宜。
2017年4月至6月，马莫松在以马某某为首的犯罪集团中，将陆某某、温某杰培养为其二级副手。在接下来的13个月里，直到2018年7月，他们通过微信集团建立了私人电影院的盗版网络。一旦私人电影院签约并连接起来，他们就授权通过远程控制软件播放盗版电影。据“新京报”报道，“两匹马与上海一家科技公司勾结，对他们的盗版电影进行加密。刘薇说，这家公司是‘一人公司’，只有一个老板/员工。“。令人啼笑皆非的是，这家科技公司的负责人竟然为他的加密技术申请了专利。每一家私人影院甚至都有一个看不见的水印，以追踪任何重新盗版拷贝的来源。
因此，私人影院无法第二次传播电影，别无选择，只能每月支付2万元人民币(2900美元)的特许经营费。虽然他们很早就获得了电影副本，但马某某禁止私人电影院在电影以合法的多厅放映之前放映。如果这部电影在早上第一次在合法的电影院放映，盗版电影院就会在当天下午一两点左右准备好拷贝。顾客只需要进入私人影院房间或摊位，在盗版自己的验证和解密在幕后进行后，订购电影并播放即可。
这场盗版行动的产业规模之大，堪比处理从销售、分销、促销到账单的一切事务的技巧，令人叹为观止。在其鼎盛时期，该团伙经营着一个分销网络，涵盖20个省份的330家私人电影院。除了收取特许经营费、设备服务费和每部电影的费用等机制外，每一台都配备了高清胶片源、播放设备和加密技术。然而，正是这种非常复杂的控制系统成为海盗团伙瓦解的一部分。
吊装。
.
note_en:
| June 6, 2019 11:38 pm PDT       It was the piracy nightmare that was supposed to be technically impossible. Hollywood studios had spent years coming up with the digital cinema specifications and a certification process that ensured military-grade encryption would protect the latest blockbuster film releases. Camcording, albeit traceable was still possible, but getting a perfect digital copy off a server would never happen.
 Except somewhere in China, digital cinema projection server No. A15591 was letting pirates make pristine HD copies the very same day new releases opened in cinemas. The piracy tracking team of China’s Central Propaganda Department’s Film Technology Quality Inspection Institute even had a nick-name for the server: Ghost No. 1. It was their job to find it – and stop it. This is the story of their three-year battle to take down the greatest film piracy ring to ever operate in China.
 Piracy Becomes A Big Business In China With China having become the world’s second biggest cinema market in terms of box office, and the world’s largest in terms of cinema screen numbers, the commercial value of film releases has soared. Not just for Hollywood and Chinese film distributors, but also for local film pirates. Far from just being amateur torrent enthusiasts, piracy is a large-scale criminal commercial enterprise, even in a tightly policed state such as Communist China. Street stalls of VCDs (video compact discs) and DVDs (digital video discs) long ago fell out of fashion with the rise of smartphones, so today links to pirated films on shadowy websites are instead traded on social media channels such as WeChat and QQ, which have conveniently built-in micro-payment systems.
  Streaming has grown even faster in China than the number of multiplexes, with the legal streaming market worth CNY ¥53.6 billion (USD $7.7 billion) in 2018 – only CNY ¥7.3 billion (USD $1 billion) less than the country’s total box office that same year. As cinema ticket subsidies in China have been reigned in, the lure of an HD copy of the latest cinema release for less than the equivalent of a dollar can be irresistible. Such an opportunity was enabled by a sophisticated shadowy network of private cinemas fed by a piracy syndicate with no regard for intellectual property (IP). And most of the HD copies being circulated could be traced back to one single source.
  Ghost No. 1, Spies and an Engineer Hero The story of the monumental effort by Chinese authorities to track down the “ghost” server has a plot worthy of a film and interestingly enough began with a spy film. The first person to come across the rogue server was Zhou Lingfei, an engineer of the Film Technology Quality Inspection Institute, on 6 December 2016 when he found a link to a pirated version of the Brad Pitt / Marilon Cottilard spy thriller “Allied” from Paramount Pictures. Image forensic testing and analysis revealed that the pirated movie’s digital watermark ID was 138471 and the serial number of the playback server to be A15591. The digital cinema server was registered to Screen 2 of Jiuzhou Studios, Xuefu Road, Jingkou District, Zhenjiang City.
 Zhou notified the Ministry of Culture, Radio and Television, but a week later the engineer was informed that the Jiuzhou Studios cinema had closed down and the equipment sold off – with the new destination unknown. But the “phantom server” was only getting started, because 20 days later five new pirated films appeared online with the same server using the identity of the Beijing Digital Theater in the Hebei, a mountainous northern Chinese province near Beijing. This time the titles weren’t just Hollywood films but also the Hong Kong action thriller “Shock Wave.” Since the server couldn’t be physically located, Zhou notified China’s three main distributors, including China Film Group and Huaxia, instructing them to blacklist the server with serial number A15591 and to block the use of its digital security certificate from the keystore for key delivery message (KDM) keys to decrypt future films.
 China is, if anything, stricter than Hollywood in its oversight of the deployment and control of digital cinema systems. With just 40 cinema operator licences to screen first-run films having been issued, it’s not possible to simply buy a projector / server combo and then strike an agreement with distributors to show their films. Each projector and server that is manufactured, no matter where they end up in the world, comes from the factory with a unique serial number. The ID and digital security certificate of each piece of equipment is held in what is known as a trusted devices list (TDL) by all distributors or content service providers (which are strictly controlled in China) and matched to specific cinema locations.
 Distributors then send a hard drive with the encrypted film (a.k.a. a digital cinema package or DCP) to each cinema in which the film is to be shown and then separately issue a KDM that “unlocks” the film for a specific server / projector combo during a set period of time. Yet even when decrypted the image carries a watermark, invisible to the human eye, that can identify the playback equipment, time and location where the film was shown – assuming the server identity is correct. But Ghost No. 1 was evading identification by what the Chinese refer to as “resurrecting the corpse,” or in more prosaic technical terms, “cloning.”
  Since 2012 Chinese authorities have used digital cinema watermarking to successfully trace and prosecute over 500 instances of film theft, including for major blockbusters such as “Painted Skin,” “Lost in Thailand” and “Wolf Warrior 2.” Huang Mingzhe, deputy detachment leader of the Public Security Defender of Yangzhou City Public Security Bureau (located just across the Yangtze River from Zhenjiang City, where the server originated from) explained how Ghost No. 1 managed to evade efforts to find it and stop it for several years, “The so-called cloning is the copying of the digital security certificate of one server of a regular theater to another server, so that the two servers have the same ID.” Chasing Ghost No. 1 just became a game of whack-a-mole; blacklist one valid security certificate and the pirates could just copy another non-blacklisted certificate onto A15591.
 (Please Note: We have purposely obscured the precise methodology so as not to provide instructional references for any would-be pirates.)
  Operation “2.15”  This year’s Chinese New Year (a.k.a. Spring Festival or Lunar New Year) holiday would prove critical for the efforts to thwart piracy in China. Historically this is a time reserved for the biggest domestic blockbusters, with big films such as “The Wandering Earth” and “Crazy Alien” block booked across tens of thousands of screens. With talk of a ‘Winter is coming’ for China’s film and cinema business seeing stagnating growth in 2019, no-one could afford box office revenue being siphoned off by pirates. But that’s apparently just what happened. Between February 4th and 10th the total box office in China was CNY ¥5.8 billion (USD $839 million) , including the one billion yuan (USD $144 million)  sci-fi hit “The Wandering Earth” earned in just four days. Though a box office record for the period,  attendance was largely flat and only higher ticket prices made it stronger than last year.  Was piracy partly to blame?
 According to the data from the  National Copyright Association of the People’s Republic of China (PRC), which had been monitoring piracy activity over the holiday, the number of infringing online links of the eight films released over the holidays numbered 51,000.  Of those, no less than 38,900 of the infringing links were to HD versions, and the total number of click-to-play broadcasts was about 82.82 million. This is estimated to have resulted in a box office loss of about CNY ¥787 million (USD $114 million). More significantly, of the 27 pirated versions of the eight films released, the source was always the same: server A15591 or Ghost No. 1.
 Chinese authorities notoriously block any Hollywood or foreign films about spirits or afterlife from having a theatrical release in China ( even the recent all-female “Ghostbusters”) so as not to “promote cults or superstition”. Now they were about to take an even harsher approach on the “Ghost” server.
 A joint operation was launched with the name “2.15,” codenamed after 15 February of the New Year Holiday period, under the auspices of the Ministry of Public Security. While the many levels of public security bodies, ministries and police departments in China tackling this might seem bewildering to outsiders, China is incredibly efficient when it comes to coordinating security actions. Even prior to the 2.15 operation the anti-piracy bodies had completed 25 cases of film and television infringement and piracy detection, in which 251 criminal suspects had been arrested, 361 pirated video websites closed, 57 apps shut down, and seven high-definition movie piracy servers seized. The total amounts involved in these actions was CNY ¥230 million (USD $33 million). But this would pale next to the hunt for Ghost No. 1.
 The first step was to infiltrate the WeChat channels used by the criminal pirate gangs to sell links to HD copies. A perfect HD copy of “The Wandering Earth” had appeared online just a day after the movie opened in cinemas, despite the efforts of the film’s producer Gong Geer to increase security around the film release, as well as having three anti-piracy teams monitoring all social media channels day and night. Based on the information culled from social media channels (the exact methods are being kept secret) five arrest teams were despatched to the cities of Anshan and Xiangtan, both in Hunan Province in South Central China, where they monitored the main suspects. Simultaneously 13 smaller units were initially sent to eight provinces and 11 cities for investigation and evidence collection. In total Yangzhou Public Security Bureau dispatched more than 200 police officers to work in 46 cities in 20 provinces to make arrests.
  Once the Ministry of Public Security gave the order the teams swooped in and made the arrests of the five key suspect targets: Ma Mou, Ma Mosong, Wen Mou, Lu Mou, and a technician with the surname Liu (no first name). There were 59 additional suspects arrested (some reports claim over 250) across the provinces of Liaoning, Heilongjiang and Hebei in the north and north east and Hunan in the south. More than 100 private cinemas were raided. A total of 13,673 devices were impounded, including 67 pirated movie hard drives and four projection servers. These had been used to provide over 10,000 films and TV shows to pirate sites, apps and shadowy ‘private cinemas’. The authorities ultimately investigated more than 1,900 suspected individuals, verified 185 key personnel, and successfully locked up five major criminal suspects of the criminal group during the 2.15 operation.
 Journalists shown around the evidence by Yangzhou Municipal Public Security Bureau saw that the physical evidence in this case was almost enough to fill an entire storage hall.
 In photographs, a large number of “dongles” could be seen piled up in a number of boxes arranged in sequence on a physical evidence shelf. The task force police officer Mr Ke picked up a small USB ‘dongle’ key and said: “This is used to install the playback equipment on the offline private cinema. Without it, the criminal group’s pirated movies can’t be released. More than 9,000 such dongles had not yet been sold or sent out. If it spreads, criminal groups will develop so many boxes that can play pirated movies. Such a large amount will have a big impact on the Chinese film market.”
 The pirates were creating a shadow network of private cinemas showing first-run films illegally. And they were able to do so thanks to one of the four servers amongst the more than 13,000 devices impounded – the notorious Ghost No. 1. Yet it is the story of how server A15591 or Ghost No. 1 came to be the fountain of pirated films that holds the most chilling lesson for the cinema industry in China and abroad. Because there is nothing to stop a Ghost No. 2 or No. 3 or so on.
   The details of how server A15591 came to be the greatest enemy of Chinese film distributors was first revealed in detail by online news portal Sohu, though with additional reporting from the likes of China News, Xhby and others. What follows was pieced together from multiple sources with the assistance of Google Translate.
 Chief suspect Ma Mou registered in June 2014 to operate a drive-in cinema in Anshan City – a steel-town in northern China twinned with UK’s Sheffield – together with former classmates and Ma Mosong. But situated north of Dalien and west of North Korea the northern climate, with its winds and dust, was not conducive for drive-in theatres. So the two former classmates Ma Mou and Ma Mosong started dreaming up new business plans for their “Two Horses” criminal gang. (“Ma” is the Chinese character for horse.) They joined forces in 2015 with local business partner and fixer Huo Molei to add on a screen to the drive-in that could show new films.
 Around this time  private cinemas (a.k.a. “micro cinemas” or “on-demand cinemas”) were starting to take off throughout China, most of them legitimate but many of them also lax to implement regulations relating to everything from fire safety to IP protection. But how could such venues gain first-mover advantage and attract customers with the latest film releases in regular multiplexes, was Ma’s problem. To obtain first-run releases a pirate high definition camera recording would be required, but this was a risky thing to do for each cinema release. Only instead of using inferior camcorded copies, it would be better to get a pristine DCP directly from the server. So, in 2017 Ma Mou paid CNY ¥50,000 (USD $7,235) for a first-generation GDC digital cinema server with the serial number A15591 from a man named Huo Mou Lei. This was a pre-DCI compliant server. Previously, Huo had started a cinema equipment dealership to sell movie playback equipment for regular cinemas and provide renovation and maintenance services.
 This was the server that had been identified the previous year as the source of pirated films and was hence officially blacklisted on TDLs for the creation of security keys (KDMs). So Ma Mou purchased a server that was effectively useless. This is where Huo Mou Lei stepped in to help resolve the problem of how to get pirated copies from the server. He contacted a technician named Liu, who had knowledge of how to “clone” a server certificate. Liu gained entry to a cinema in Tang County, Hebei Province under the pretext of “equipment maintenance,” secretly copied the digital certificate of its server and downloaded the account and password for the KDM storage server. Thus Ghost No.1 was back in business with the newly cloned security certificate with serial number A03783. Thus once the watermark was forensically extracted from the pirated video file it would not be traced back to Ghost No. 1, since the invisible watermark in the image would reveal the registered origin of the playback equipment based on the TDL.
 With the equipment issue resolved, it was time to get their hands on some content. The Two Horses checked the Maoyan ticket app for the three upcoming releases with the highest audience scores. They then partnered with Wang Moufei, head of projection at a multiplex in Anshen City, who became their supplier of DCPs taken from hard drives sent legitimately by distributors. For this the Two Horses paid him CNY ¥500-1,000 (USD $75-$150) per month to borrow hard drives for up to 10 films. Back in Ma Mou’s studio the gang used professional HD cameras and sound cards to record the films and used video editing software to tweak, correct and sync the finished film file.
  The Pirate Cinema Network Rather than selling physical copies (DVDs) or streaming them online, Ma Mou and Ma Mosong decided to target private cinemas. By the end of 2017 there were estimated to be over 8,000 such “on-demand cinemas” across China, many of which did not respect copyright when it came to competing for customers. The gang adopted the traditional marketing method of contacting the heads of shadowy private cinemas, introduced their business, then offered them a pirated sample to play. They “established a black industrial chain of pirate cinema film production, distribution and encryption management,” in the words of Zhang Zuoliang, deputy director of the Public Security Administration of the Ministry of Public Security.
 Knowing that there is no honour among thieves, Ma Mou and Ma Mosong ensured that their pirated films did not get re-pirated by establishing a system similar to that of legitimately licensed cinemas to monitor offline use by adding watermarks, encryption, and transmission to the networked disk storage. “In two years, we have made a total of more than 200 HD pirated movies,” suspect No. 2 Ma Mosong confessed to the police. Advertising and communication was done over WeChat where reporters were shown an exchange between the pirate gang and a private cinema owner who complained about the cost of equipment for his 13-room private cinema. The Two Horses contact replied, “The monthly fee is ¥3,000 yuan (USD $434). The first time you have to buy an encrypted disk. Encrypted disks will be delivered by a courier, and the per-room cost is ¥500 yuan (USD $72), you can re-use it later. Then the usage fee is ¥100 yuan (USD $14.50) a month. No, the encrypted disk can be returned, with a refund of ¥300 yuan (USD $43).” Private cinemas that purchased these pirated HD films have in turn advertised them to customers with: “When you watch a movie, 2 people only need ¥98 yuan (USD $14.18),” thus working out cheaper than a cinema.
 Between April and June 2017 Ma Mosong groomed Lu Mou and Wen Moujie as his second-tier lieutenants in the criminal group headed by Ma Mou. For the next 13 months, until July 2018, they built out their pirate network of private cinemas through WeChat groups. Once private cinemas had signed up and been connected, they authorized pirated films to be played via remote control software.  According to Beijing News, “Two Horses colluded with a Shanghai technology company to encrypt their pirated movies. Liu Wei said that this company is a ‘one-person company’ and there is only one boss/employee.” It is no small irony that the head of this tech company even applied for a patent for his encryption technology. There was even an invisible watermark for each private cinema, to trace the source of any re-pirated pirate copy.
 The private cinemas were thus unable to spread the films a second time and had no choice but to pay a monthly CNY ¥20,000 (USD $2,900) franchise fee. Though they got access to the film copies early, Ma Mou banned the private cinemas from screening the films before they had been released in legitimate multiplexes. If the film was first shown in a legitimate cinema in the morning, the pirate cinemas would have a copy ready to go around one or two o’clock that same afternoon. Customers just needed to enter the private cinema room or booth, order up the film and play it after the pirate’s own verification and decryption took place behind the scenes.
 The sheer industrial scale of this piracy operation is as breathtaking as the finesse with which everything from sales, distribution, promotion and billing was handled. At its height the gang operated a distribution network that encompassed 330 private cinemas in 20 provinces. Each one of these had been equipped with HD film sources, playback devices and encryption technology, in addition to the mechanisms for collecting franchise fees, equipment service fees, per-film fees and more. Yet it was this very elaborate control system that became part of the pirate gangs undoing.
  Hoisted
......

“一张六人桌吗？不是的，先生，那是违反新冠肺炎的<English>…</English>限制的。除非你保证你的党讨论的是生意，而不是娱乐。“。
伦敦一些最豪华的餐厅在二级冠状病毒封锁限制中发现了一个漏洞，该限制旨在防止家庭混合，从而减缓病毒的传播。
政府规定允许自由职业者在午餐时间工作的一项豁免，正被高端餐厅所利用，这些餐厅鼓励多达30人一起用餐，只要“主题是商务”。
由千万富翁理查德·凯利(Richard Careing)经营的餐饮帝国Caprice Holdings本周向忠实客户发送电子邮件，邀请他们预订似乎打破了单一家庭规则的预订。
电子邮件中写道：“我们希望说明--当话题是商务时，你们仍然可以在没有‘单户规定’(即混合家庭最多允许6名客人)的限制下，共进午餐或晚餐，共进一顿令人难以置信的工作午餐或晚餐。”“我们可以在我们的私人用餐空间内招待多达30人参加商务会议。”
肉桂俱乐部因其位于威斯敏斯特宫附近而广受政界人士和游说者的欢迎，该俱乐部表示：“很明显，如果你是出于商务目的来到我们的餐厅，你们可以在午餐或晚餐时见面，而不受‘单户’的限制，这是允许最多6名客人的。”
D&amp；D London拥有高端的Quaglino‘s、Coq d’Argent、蓝鸟咖啡馆和德国体育馆，该公司表示：“我们很高兴地确认，您仍然可以在午餐和晚餐期间举行商务会议。”如果您预订的主要目的是商务，我们将一如既往地欢迎您的光临。“。
Corbin&amp；King的共同所有者杰里米·金(Jeremy King)给自己喜欢的客户发电子邮件告诉他们：“虽然社交场合需要来自单一家庭，但已经确定商务会议是可以接受的。”Corbin&amp；King拥有商务人群最喜欢的酒店，包括滨海的Delaunay酒店和皮卡迪利的Wolseley酒店。
尽管电梯容量有限，但碎片大厦和苍鹭大厦的几家餐厅正在接受商务午餐的大额预订。利物浦街站旁苍鹭大厦(Heron Tower)40层的鸭子华夫饼(Duck&Amp；Waffle)将接受6人的商务午餐预订，而楼下两层的寿司桑巴(Sushi Samba)则乐于在午餐时最多14人讨论电子表格。
这些餐厅依赖于2020年健康保护(冠状病毒，当地新冠肺炎警戒级别)(中等)法规中的“例外3”，其中规定：“例外3是聚会是合理必要的-a)出于工作目的或提供志愿或慈善服务。”
这些信息似乎起作用了。伦敦许多最著名的餐厅，包括位于SOHO的Carey‘s帝国的一部分常春藤餐厅(The Ivy)和伦敦金融城的Le Coq d’Argent餐厅，周五午餐时间生意火爆。
在梅菲尔伯克利广场(Berkeley Square)上，Careing最新的昂贵鱼餐厅Sexy Fish里，所有的桌子都坐满了人，迫使不请自来的人和“卫报”(The Guardian)的记者一样，在酒吧用餐，酒吧里装饰着达米恩·赫斯特(Damien Hirst)的青铜美人鱼雕塑。
细读菜单，包括GB42的帝王蟹和鱼子酱寿司，一块可以用1.6万GB的Armand de Brignac香槟喝下去，有三组四人，似乎不是来自同一个家庭。
一起用餐的四位女士表示，她们正在“某种程度上”共进商务午餐，因为她们“是前同事”，正在叙旧。面对进一步的挑战，其中一人表示，她“很高兴打破规则，因为我知道我不会伤害任何人，否则我不会”。
这两名女子说，她们没有预订，但走进餐厅，服务员没有问她们是否来自不同的家庭。餐厅经理要求卫报记者离开餐厅，他说他意识到四个人的三张桌子违反了规定。
他说：“我们会提醒每一位客人，我们会在确认电子邮件中提醒他们。”“我们尽我们所能做…。我们正在尽最大努力，我们正在做尽可能多的预防措施…。你不能问我一个问题。“
政府内部似乎对谁可以澄清商务午餐的官方立场感到困惑。财政部建议成立商业、能源和工业策略部(Department for Business，Energy and Industrial Strategy)。Beis反过来又指向了更高的权威。
唐宁街表示，大多数跨户工作餐都是不允许的，只有在别无选择的情况下才应该这样做。但一位发言人表示，政府不太可能对推广商务午餐的餐厅采取任何行动。
“我们显然鼓励人们在可能的情况下在工作会议上使用其他选择，比如Covid安全的工作场所，或者通过其他方式-虚拟的或电话的，”一位10号发言人说。“我们本周已经阐述了为什么引入这项豁免，以便让自由职业者和其他可能无法进入Covid安全工作场所的人使用。但我们鼓励每个人都采取负责任的行动。“
note_en:
“A table for six? No, sir, that is against the Covid-19 restrictions … unless you promise that your party will discuss business, not pleasure.”
 Some of London’s fanciest restaurants have discovered a loophole in the  tier-2 coronavirus lockdowns restrictions designed to prevent households from mixing and thereby slow the spread of the virus.
 An exemption that the government included in the rules to allow freelancers to work over lunch is being exploited by high-end restaurants encouraging up to 30 people to dine together as long as “the topic is business”.
  Caprice Holdings, the restaurant empire run by multimillionaire Richard Caring, emailed loyal clients this week to invite them to make bookings that appear to break the single-household rule.
 “We wish to make it clear – when the topic is business, you can still meet over a fabulous working lunch or dinner without the restriction of the ‘single household rule’, ie mixed households are permitted up to six guests,” the email said. “We can host up to 30 people within our private dining spaces for business meetings.”
 The Cinnamon Club, popular with politicians and lobbyists due to its location near the Palace of Westminster, said: “It’s become clear that if you are coming down to our restaurants for business purposes, you can meet over lunch or dinner without the ‘single household’ restriction and this is permitted for up to six guests.”
 D&D  London, which owns the upmarket Quaglino’s, Coq d’Argent, the Bluebird Café and the German Gymnasium, said: “We are pleased to confirm that you can still have business meetings over lunch and dinner. Provided that the primary purpose of your booking is business, we are delighted to welcome you as usual.”
 Jeremy King, co-owner of Corbin & King, which owns business crowd favourites including the Delaunay on the Strand and the Wolseley in Piccadilly, emailed favoured clients to tell them: “Whilst social occasions need to be from a single household, it has been determined that business meeting are acceptable.”
 Despite restricted elevator capacity, several restaurants in the Shard and Heron towers are accepting big bookings for business lunches. Duck & Waffle, on the 40th floor of Heron Tower by Liverpool Street station, will take bookings for six for a business lunch, while Sushi Samba two floors below is happy for up to 14 people to discuss spreadsheets over lunch.
 The restaurants are relying on  “exception 3” in the Health Protection (Coronavirus, Local Covid-19 Alert Level) (Medium) Regulations 2020 Act, which states: “Exception 3 is that the gathering is reasonably necessary – a) for work purposes or for the provision of voluntary or charitable services.”
 The messages appear to have worked. Many of London’s most famous restaurants, including the Ivy, part of Caring’s empire in Soho, and Le Coq d’Argent in the City, were doing a roaring trade on Friday lunchtime.
 At Sexy Fish, Caring’s latest pricey fish restaurant on Berkeley Square in Mayfair, all the tables were occupied, forcing walk-ins, like reporters from the Guardian, to dine at the bar, adorned with  bronze mermaids sculptures by Damien Hirst.
 Perusing the menu, which includes king crab and caviar sushi at £42 a piece which can be washed down with a £16,000 Armand de Brignac champagne, were three groups of four who did not appear to be from the same household.
 A group of four ladies dining together said they were “sort of” having a business lunch as they “are ex-colleagues” catching up. Challenged further, one of the party said she was “happy to break the rules as I know I am not going to be doing anyone any harm, otherwise I wouldn’t”.
 The women said they did not book, but walked into the restaurant and were not asked by the maitre d’ if they were from separate households. The restaurant manager, who asked the Guardian reporter to leave the restaurant, said he realised that the three tables of four people broke the rules.
 “We alert that to every guest and we do that on confirmation email,” he said. “We do as much as we possibly can … We are doing our absolute best, we’re doing as many precautions as possible … You can’t ask me a single question.”
  There seemed to be confusion in the government about who could clarify the official position on business lunches. The Treasury suggested the Department for Business, Energy and Industrial Strategy. BEIS, in turn, pointed to a higher authority.
 Downing Street said most cross-household work meals were not permitted, and that they should only happen if there was no other option. But a spokesman indicated that the government was unlikely to take any action against restaurants promoting business lunches.
 “We obviously encourage people to use alternatives for work meetings where possible, like Covid-secure workplaces, or through other means – virtually or on the phone,” a No 10 spokesman said. “We have set out this week why the exemption was introduced, in order to allow freelancers and others who maybe don’t have access to a Covid-secure workplace to use. But we encourage everyone to act responsibly.”
title: 伦敦的顶级餐厅正在歪曲Covid规则
title_en: London's top restaurants are skewering the Covid rules

美国太平洋西北部的沿海居民是(或者应该是！)。熟悉“大地震”的意义--某一天将在近海发生的大地震。构造板块边界，也就是洋壳向下滑动到北美大陆下方的地方，能够产生大的震动和令人难以置信的危险的海啸。这是几个世纪以来没有发生过的，导致了一种错误的安全感，但证据就在那里，证明了过去更深层次的事件。
但是板块边界本身并不是地震危险的唯一来源。例如，在波特兰附近，有许多较小的活动断层。最近几年甚至还发生了中度地震，以起到提醒作用。1993年，该市以南仅50公里(31英里)处发生5.7级地震，造成约3000万美元的损失。在这里，地震学家也可以更深入地研究过去。这表明更大的地震是有可能发生的。
今天，我们用地震仪测量和量化地震，但有可能找到证明过去地震的物理证据。古地震学依赖于这样一个事实，即有些东西在地震中移动，使它们的位置移位。在合适的情况下，您甚至可以看到故障移动了多少，并为每个事件指定了日期。这使得可以粗略估计地震强度，以及两次大地震之间的平均时间。(请注意，这并不意味着我们可以预测下一次会议的日期。地震不是钟表事件。)。
在洋壳潜入俄勒冈州下方的地方，两个板块显然正在碰撞。但看看西海岸本身，构造力量要复杂得多。这块大陆实际上有点旋转，所以俄勒冈州在与大洋板块相撞时向西北偏北方向滑动了一点。实际上，华盛顿做这件事的速度比俄勒冈州慢了一点，造成了中间的交通拥堵。
note_en:
Coastal denizens of the US Pacific Northwest are (or should be!) familiar with the significance of “the Big One”—a major earthquake just off the coast that will occur someday. The tectonic plate boundary, where oceanic crust slides downward beneath the North American continent, is capable of producing major shaking and an incredibly dangerous tsunami. This hasn’t happened  in centuries, leading to a false sense of security, but the evidence is there for events deeper in the past.
 But the plate boundary itself isn’t the only source of seismic danger. Near Portland, for example, there are a number of smaller active faults. There have even been moderate earthquakes in recent years to serve as reminders. In 1993, a magnitude 5.7 quake just 50 kilometers (31 miles) south of the city caused around $30 million in damage. Here, too, seismologists can look deeper into the past. And it shows that much bigger earthquakes are possible.
  Today, we measure and quantify earthquakes with seismometers, but it’s possible to find physical evidence that testifies to past earthquakes. Paleoseismology relies on the fact that some things move during an earthquake, displacing their position. In the right situation, you can even see about how much the fault moved and put a date on each event. That allows for a rough estimate of earthquake magnitude, as well as the average time between major earthquakes. (Note that this does  not mean we can predict the date of the next one. Earthquakes are not clockwork events.)
 Where the oceanic crust dives beneath Oregon, the two plates are obviously colliding. But looking at the West Coast itself, the tectonic forces are a little more complex. The continent is actually rotating a bit, such that Oregon is sliding to the north-northwest a bit as it collides with the oceanic plate. Washington is actually doing this a little more slowly than Oregon, creating a traffic-jam-squeeze in the middle.
title: 除了“大断层”，更近的断层也可能撼动波特兰。
title_en: Besides the “Big One,” closer faults could also shake Portland
